chunk_summary: Guidance on recognizing and managing allergic reactions during complementary feeding, emphasizing severity triage, emergency response with epinephrine, and follow-up care.
learning_objectives: Understand the importance of dietary diversity in complementary feeding and its role in allergen exposure.
Identify mild versus moderate-to-severe allergic reactions in infants and young children.
Learn the first-line treatment protocol for anaphylaxis, including epinephrine dosing and administration.
Develop an emergency response plan for suspected food allergies during complementary feeding.
key_facts: Complementary feeding introduces common allergens such as peanut, egg, dairy, wheat, and fish.
Mild reactions include localized hives, itching, or mild perioral rash, while severe reactions involve breathing difficulty, wheeze, stridor, or multi-system symptoms.
First-line treatment for anaphylaxis is intramuscular epinephrine at 0.01 mg/kg of 1 mg/mL (1:1000) injected into the anterolateral thigh.
Epinephrine dosing can be repeated at short intervals if symptoms persist while arranging emergency care.
Antihistamines are adjuncts and should not delay epinephrine administration or emergency referral.
Post-anaphylaxis care includes specialist evaluation, documentation of the trigger food, and a written avoidance and re-introduction plan.
reference_quote: Intramuscular epinephrine (adrenaline) is the first-line treatment for anaphylaxis. Recommended pediatric dosing is 0.01 mg/kg of 1 mg/mL (1:1000) injected into the anterolateral thigh.
